event: First relapse had been treated with allogeneic HSCT from an unrelated HLA-compatible donor
Referred to the University Children's Hospital Tuebingen for treatment with blinatumomab after a second relapse of pre-B-ALL
Antibiotic chemoprophylaxis with ceftriaxone and teicoplanin
Patient complained about pain in the left flank
Continuous infusion of morphine (max. 15 μg/kg BW per hour)
The pain aggravated on day +5 of blinatumomab treatment
Ultrasound scan did not show any pathology apart from the known hepatosplenomegaly
Suddenly on day +6 the boy seemed somnolent
Suddenly on day +6 the boy seemed sleepy
An overdose of morphine was assumed
After dose reduction the boy reacted with delay
After dose reduction the boy only opened his eyes when addressed
Neurological condition of the patient worsened again
The boy was transferred to the intensive care unit
Echocardiography showed multiple thrombi in the left and right ventricle
Thromboembolic events were presumed as the cause of the cerebral lesions
An endocarditis with multiple septic embolisms was suspected
Antibiotic regimen was intensified with meropenem, teicoplanin, and gentamicin
CT scans of the thorax, abdomen and pelvis were performed
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in both lungs revealed on CT
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in the heart revealed on CT
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in both kidneys revealed on CT
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in the liver revealed on CT
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in the intestines revealed on CT
Multiple thromboembolic lesions with ischemia, bleeding and infarction in multiple muscles revealed on CT
A bone marrow aspiration showed bone marrow aplasia with lymphatic blasts
A few hours later, the patient succumbed to cerebral herniation
The patient died in the intensive care unit seven days after starting blinatumomab treatment (day +7)
Autopsy showed an invasive mycosis of Rhizomucor pusillus as the cause of death
Universal fungal PCR of the tracheal secretion could not detect any fungal infection, even on the day the patient died
Bacterial and fungal cultures during the patient's stay did not identify any organisms
